revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:有職]][[zh:有職]]
'''Geki Nikki''' (外記日記) refers to the records and diaries written by the {{ill|Geki Bureau|ja|外記局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki Bureau|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 外記|1=geki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11430536}}, an administrative office of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|qid=Q30253177}}. As an official governmental diary, it served as an essential reference for the compilation of {{ill|National Histories|ja|国史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Histories|1=National Histories|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420601}}, the administration of government affairs, and the execution of {{ill|Annual Court Rituals|ja|年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual Court Rituals|1=Annual event|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483816}} and ceremonies.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008">Saiki, ed. (1990), p. 10</ref> It was maintained as an official diary of the Imperial Court from the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
== History ==
=== Background === Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, many government documents were created and implemented, and records were preserved and organized in each government office of the Imperial Court for future reference.<ref>Matsuzono (1997), pp. 262-265</ref><ref name="unnamed-20230316115008"/> Among these, the Geki Bureau was also tasked with record-keeping. An early instance of '''Geki Nikki''' is found in the early 9th century, when the Geki Bureau was instructed to record the '''rituals within the Imperial Palace (内裏)''' along with the '''Naiki (内記)''' of the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|qid=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center}}. As the importance of '''palace ceremonies and governmental affairs''' increased in the 9th century, the volume of records grew accordingly, leading to an expansion of the '''record-keeping duties of the Geki Bureau'''.<ref>For details on the background, see Matsuzono (1997), pp. 262-265</ref>
See also: {{ill|Geki|ja|外記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki|1=geki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11430536}}
=== Development and Use === '''Geki Nikki''' was primarily written from the late '''Nara period''' through the '''Heian period''' and was stored in government archives.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008"/> In '''Kōnin 6 (815)''', an '''imperial decree (宣旨)''' formally granted it '''official recognition as a public diary'''.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-2">Hashimoto (1976), p. 392</ref> From that point on, Geki officials recorded '''Sesshō (節会, court banquets) and temporary ceremonies''' for future reference.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-2"/>
In '''Ninna 2 (886)''', the Geki Bureau was tasked with '''recording personnel affairs''' of the Imperial Court, further solidifying the '''importance of Geki Nikki'''.<ref>Nakano (1996), p. 49</ref> Over time, it continued to be maintained as a '''public diary''' and was regarded as more significant than other official diaries.<ref>Hashimoto (1976)</ref> While '''original texts''' of Geki Nikki '''rarely survive today''',<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-3">Hashimoto (1976), p. 393</ref> '''fragments (逸文)''' can be found in documents such as the '''Hirohashi manuscript (広橋本)''' of the '''Tōgū Genpuku-ki (東宮元服記)''', held at the {{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|en|National Museum of Japanese History|qid=Q3330690|lt=National Museum of Japanese History|1=National Museum of Japanese History}}.<ref>Yoshioka Masayuki, "Geki Nikki," in *Heian Jidai-shi Jiten*, Kadokawa Shoten</ref>
The diary contained '''daily records (日次記)''' and '''specialized entries (別記)''' on events such as '''Imperial visits (行幸)''' and '''Grand Purification (大祓)'''. During ceremonies, '''cloistered officials (蔵人)''' also recorded '''tenjō nikki (殿上日記, court diaries)''' alongside Geki Nikki.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-3"/> Additionally, because it was an '''official diary''', records often contained '''similar wording and details across different time periods'''.<ref>Hashimoto (1976), p. 391</ref>
Geki Nikki was also used in the '''compilation of official national histories (国史)''' following '''Shoku Nihongi (続日本紀)'''.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008"/> The *Honchōseiki (本朝世紀)*, compiled by {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinori|en|Fujiwara no Michinori|qid=Q711600|lt=Shinsei (Fujiwara no Michinori)|1=Fujiwara no Michinori}} (Fujiwara no Michinori) under the order of {{ill|Emperor Toba|en|Emperor Toba|qid=Q349284|lt=Emperor Toba|1=Emperor Toba}}, is known to have '''extensively relied on Geki Nikki'''.<ref>Sakamoto (2020), p. 63</ref>
=== Decline === As an '''official government record''', Geki Nikki was initially restricted to the '''Geki Bureau archives''' and not permitted to circulate externally. However, '''by the 10th century''', records had already begun '''leaking outside the bureau'''.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-4">Nakano (1996), pp. 49-50</ref>
The '''loss and dispersal''' of Geki Nikki were attributed to its growing importance in '''Heian aristocratic society'''—nobles '''removed copies for transcription''', which led to its gradual dissipation.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-4"/> By the '''late 10th century''', the '''bureau’s document preservation function declined''', and in '''Chiryaku 2 (1066)''', a major '''theft of approximately 200 volumes''' of Geki Nikki was carried out by '''scribes from the Imperial Library (図書寮)'''.<ref>Nakano (1996), pp. 59-60</ref>
Additionally, the '''actual writing''' of Geki Nikki was carried out by '''lower-Ranking clerks (史生)''' beneath the Geki officials.<ref>Matsuzono (1997), pp. 274-276</ref><ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-5">Nakano (1996), pp. 52-53</ref> However, due to '''delays in their salaries''', transcription efforts ceased. '''For a time, lower-Ranking Geki (六位外記) replaced them, but this also eventually ceased'''.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-5"/>
By the '''late 11th century''', '''Geki Nikki as an official diary had ceased to exist'''.<ref>Nakano (1996), pp. 49-52</ref><ref>In Kyūan 3 (1147), Fujiwara no Yorinaga, who was serving as '''Ichijō and Kurodo-betto''', attempted to revive Geki Nikki, but the effort was unsuccessful (Hashimoto Yoshihiko, "Geki Nikki," *Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 5*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985).</ref>
Previously, scholars believed that the '''rise of private diaries (私日記)''' reduced the need for '''public diaries like Geki Nikki'''.<ref>Hashimoto (1976), p. 398</ref> However, the '''causal relationship remains debated'''—whether the '''emergence of private diaries led to Geki Nikki's decline''' or whether the '''decline of public diaries''' necessitated the '''creation of private diaries'''.<ref>Matsuzono (1997), pp. 320-321</ref>
=== Private Diaries of Geki Officials === Some records titled '''"Geki Nikki"''' were actually '''private diaries (私日記) or family records (家記)''' of Geki officials.<ref name="unnamed-20230316115008-3"/> Unlike the original '''Geki Nikki''', which was preserved in the '''Geki Bureau archives''', these private diaries were '''passed down within families'''.<ref>Matsuzono (1977), pp. 289-290</ref>
Private Geki diaries began appearing in the '''late 10th century''', and by the '''11th century''', records of '''Dai-Geki (大外記, senior Geki officials)''' became common. By the '''12th century''', other Geki officials also kept diaries.<ref>Matsuzono (1977), pp. 287-290</ref>
Examples of such records include: - *Geki Nikki Shinshō* (外記日記 新抄), written in the '''mid-Kamakura period''' - *Kyōroku 2 Geki Nikki* (享禄二年外記日記), written in the '''16th century''' by {{ill|Kiyohara no Gyōken|ja|清原業賢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara no Gyōken|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - *Dai-Geki Nakahara Morotaka’s Diary* (大外記中原師廉記)
These documents provide '''valuable insights into the workings of the Imperial Court''' and '''lower-Ranking officials''' at different points in history.<ref>Endō (2020), pp. 287-303</ref><ref>Kaneko Taku, Endō Tamaki, Kurushima Noriko, Kumizu Toshikazu, Maruyama Hiroyuki, "The *Dai-Geki Nakahara Morotaka Diary* Held by the Shiryō Hensanjo," *Tokyo University Historical Research Bulletin 23* (2013)</ref>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * See original entry for full references.
== See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku|qq=}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial rituals|qq=}} * {{ill|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|qq=}} * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|qq=}} * {{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|en|Zenken Kojitsu|qid=Q11397827|lt=Zenken Kojitsu|1=Zenken Kojitsu}} * {{ill|Historical verification|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|qq=}}
}}
{{translated page|ja|外記日記|version=104536032|comment=Imported full ja history}}
